title: WorkbookDoc basics
content: Start the workbook with '= Workbook Name' and start each sheet with '== Sheet Name'.
The canonical default is the anchored row line '@B5 | a | b | c'.
Use explicit '[cell ...]' only when the compact row form would be awkward or lossy.
Emit literal WorkbookDoc text, not JSON unicode escapes such as '\u0022', '\u0027', '\u003E', or '\u003C'.

title: Workbook attributes
content: Workbook-level settings use AsciiDoc-style attribute lines such as ':wbdoc: 4' and ':active: Summary'.
Built-in workbook attributes are ':wbdoc:', ':locale:', ':date-system:', ':recalc:', and ':active:'.
':wbdoc:' is required for canonical files, ':date-system:' may be 1900 or 1904, ':recalc:' may be auto or manual, and ':active:' must match a sheet name when present.

title: Shared style directive
content: Define a reusable style with '[style <name> <style-entry>...]'.
Example: '[style title bold fg=#FFFFFF bg=#4472C4 align=center/middle]'.
Built-in style entries are fmt, fg, bg, bold, italic, underline, strike, font, size, align, wrap, clip, overflow, border, locked, and hide-formula.

title: Named ranges and formulas
content: Define a named range or named formula with '[name <name> = <reference-or-formula>]'.
Examples: '[name TaxRate = Inputs!B2]' and '[name ActiveSales = =FILTER(Data!A2:F200,Data!G2:G200="Active")]'.
Shared directives normally appear before the first sheet.

title: Sheet headers
content: Each sheet starts with '== Sheet Name'.
Sheet directives usually appear below the heading and before the first row line.
The built-in sheet surface is intentionally small and keeps only common open-state or interpretation hints.

title: Sheet state
content: Use '[state hidden]' when a sheet exists in the workbook but should not be shown by default.
Built-in value: 'hidden'.
This is a portable sheet visibility directive, not a provider-specific very-hidden feature.

title: Sheet view
content: Use '[view <entry>...]' for first-open viewing hints.
Built-in entries are 'freeze=<rows>,<cols>', 'zoom=<n>', and 'grid'.
Examples: '[view freeze=1,1 zoom=90 grid]' and '[view freeze=5,2]'.

title: Used range
content: Use '[used <range>]' for the intended occupied rectangle of the sheet.
This is important when meaningful content starts away from A1, outer blank framing is intentionally omitted, or stable preview bounds matter.
Examples: '[used B3:H50]' and '[used A1:K200]'.

title: Not built in
content: The built-in surface intentionally excludes tab color, exact column widths, exact row heights, page or print layout, saved filter state, images, protection, theme palettes, provider-specific chart extras, and macros.
Those details are not part of the built-in WorkbookDoc surface because they are less useful than semantic grid content, formulas, conditional formatting, and charts for agent-facing text.
The spec index focuses on built-in WorkbookDoc features only.

title: Addressing and ranges
content: WorkbookDoc uses A1 notation everywhere.

Supported forms include a single cell like 'B3', a rectangle like 'B5:H50', a whole column like 'B:B', a whole row like '5:5', a quoted sheet name like ''North America''!B2', and absolute references like '$B$5:$H$50'.
Use the same address syntax for rows, merges, fmt ranges, type ranges, charts, sparklines, conditional formatting, and pivots.

title: Anchored row lines
content: Syntax: '@<address> [<row-attrlist>] | <cell> | <cell> | <cell> ...'.
The address is the first cell of the row and later cell addresses are inferred by position.
Use 'blank' for internal gaps, omit trailing blank cells, and omit fully blank rows unless a later row anchor needs to preserve the vertical gap.

title: Row attrlists
content: The optional attrlist after the row anchor applies defaults to every cell in that row.
Example: '@B5 [.hdr] | Project Name | Task Name | Assigned to'.
Use row attrlists for full-row patterns such as header rows instead of repeating the same cell attrs inline.

title: Cell attrlists
content: Inline cell attrlist shape: '[.style-name key=value flag] cell-content'.
Built-in entries are .style-name, fmt, fg, bg, bold, italic, underline, strike, font, size, align, wrap, clip, overflow, border, link, result, span, locked, and hide-formula.

Use 'fmt=' for number formats, not 'numfmt='. Border syntax is 'border=side:line:color' or 'border=all:line:color'. Writers should omit attrs that only restate defaults, especially default colors and default alignment such as 'align=left/middle'.

title: Cell content forms
content: Built-in content forms are numbers like '1250', booleans 'true' and 'false', 'blank', formulas like '=SUM(B2:B10)', typed literals like 'date("2026-04-18")', errors like '#DIV/0!', plain text, quoted text, and rich text like 'rich("Status: [b]At Risk[/b]")'.
Use plain text for the normal case and quote only when the token would otherwise parse as another literal kind or must preserve whitespace or pipes.
Cell tokens and explicit '[cell ...]' overrides share the same content forms.

title: Formula cached results
content: Keep the formula itself as the cell content and store the last displayed value with an optional 'result=...' attr.
Examples: '[result=1250] =SUM(B2:B10)' and '[cell C8 result=154200] =SUM(Data!B2:B5)'.
This cache form is optional and mainly useful for Excel-to-WorkbookDoc fidelity; WorkbookDoc-to-Excel writers may ignore it.

title: Quoted strings
content: Use double quotes when text needs leading or trailing spaces, a literal '|', a leading '=', a reserved literal such as 'blank', a line break, or content that would otherwise parse as another literal kind.
Supported escapes are '\"', '\\', '\n', '\t', and '\|'.
Use quoted strings only when needed; plain text is preferred for the compact common case.

title: Explicit cell overrides
content: Syntax: '[cell <address> <entry>...] [literal]'.
Examples: '[cell C8 result=154200 style=currency] =SUM(Data!B2:B5)', '[cell D8 rich="Status: [b][color=#C00000]At Risk[/color][/b]"]', and '[cell E8 link="https://example.com"] "Open"'.
Use '[cell ...]' for one-off cases such as a formula with cached result, awkward rich text, a blank cell with meaningful metadata, or an exact per-cell override in an otherwise compact sheet.

title: Inline horizontal span
content: For short horizontal merges inside a row, use 'span=<n>' on the owning cell.
Example: '@B3 [.title span=7] | "Project Management Data"'.
Covered cells are omitted from the row line.

title: Merge directive
content: Use '[merge <range> [align=horizontal[/vertical]]]' for any merge, especially multi-row merges.
The top-left cell owns the value and formula.
Use 'align=' on the merge when the merge itself implies presentation, for example '[merge B3:H3 align=center/middle]'.

title: Type ranges
content: Use '[type <range> <kind>]' when adjacent cells repeat the same explicit temporal kind.
Built-in kinds are 'date', 'time', and 'datetime'.
When a type range is present, cells in that range should usually use raw values such as '2026-04-18' instead of repeating 'date("2026-04-18")' on every cell.

title: Shared format ranges
content: Use '[fmt <range> <style-entry>...]' when repeating the same non-default inline attrs across adjacent cells would be noisy.
Examples: '[fmt B5:H5 bold bg=#D9E2F3]' and '[fmt H6:H50 fmt="0.0%" align=right/middle]'.

It uses the same built-in style entries as cell attrlists, so use 'fmt=' rather than 'numfmt=' and use border syntax such as 'border=all:thin:#D9D9D9'. Inline cell attrs or explicit '[cell ...]' details override '[fmt ...]'.

title: Conditional formatting overview
content: Syntax: '[cf <range> <rule> <format-entry>...]'.
Use conditional formatting when formatting depends on cell values, text, formulas, scales, bars, or icon sets.
Built-in format entries are 'priority=<n>', 'stop', 'fill=#RRGGBB', 'fg=#RRGGBB', 'bold', 'italic', and 'underline'. Providers may approximate rule families they cannot render natively.

title: Conditional format rule: when cell
content: Rule form: 'when cell <value-op> value'.
Meaning: formatting depends on the cell's own value compared against a threshold.
Example: '[cf H6:H50 when cell > 0.15 fill=#C6EFCE fg=#006100]'.

title: Conditional format rule: when text contains
content: Rule form: 'when text contains "..."'.
Meaning: formatting depends on whether the cell text contains a substring.
Example: '[cf C2:C100 when text contains "Risk" fg=#C00000 bold]'.

title: Conditional format rule: when formula
content: Rule form: 'when formula(...)'.
Meaning: formatting depends on a formula evaluated relative to the top-left cell of the target range.
Example: '[cf H6:H50 when formula(=H6<0.5) fill=#FFF2CC]'.

title: Conditional format rule: scale
content: Rule form: 'scale(...)'.
Meaning: apply a color scale across the whole range.
Example: '[cf I9:I78 scale(min:#63BE7B,50%:#FCFCFF,max:#F8696B)]'.

title: Conditional format rule: data bar
content: Rule form: 'data-bar(...)'.
Meaning: show an in-cell bar whose length reflects the value.
Example: '[cf H6:H50 data-bar(color=#5B9BD5)]'.

title: Conditional format rule: icon set
content: Rule form: 'icon-set(...)'.
Meaning: show an icon chosen from thresholds or buckets.
Example: '[cf H6:H50 icon-set(3-arrows)]'.

title: Table directive
content: Syntax: '[table <name> <range> <entry>...]'.
Built-in entries are 'filter', 'totals', 'banded', and 'style=<name>'.
Use it when a normal cell rectangle should behave like an Excel or Sheets data table, for example '[table ProjectTasks B5:H50 filter banded]'.

title: Pivot directive
content: Syntax: '[pivot <name> source=<source> at=<cell>]' followed by pivot lines and a closing '[end]'.
Built-in pivot line kinds are '- row <field>', '- col <field>', '- val <field> <agg>', and '- filter <field>'.

Use pivots when a sheet contains an aggregated pivot view derived from a source range or table. 'source=' must point to a table name or to a range whose first row is the header row used by the pivot field names.

title: Pivot line kind: row
content: Line kind: '- row <field>'.
Meaning: add a row grouping field.
Example: '- row Region'.

title: Pivot line kind: col
content: Line kind: '- col <field>'.
Meaning: add a column grouping field.
Example: '- col Quarter'.

title: Pivot line kind: val
content: Line kind: '- val <field> <agg> [as "<label>"]'.
Meaning: add an aggregated value field.
Example: '- val Revenue sum as "Sum of Revenue"'.

title: Pivot line kind: filter
content: Line kind: '- filter <field>'.
Meaning: add a pivot filter field.
Example: '- filter Status'.

title: Chart directive
content: Syntax: '[chart "<name>" type=<type> at=<cell> size=<width>x<height><unit>]' followed by '- series ...' lines and a closing '[end]'.
Built-in chart types are column, bar, line, area, pie, doughnut, scatter, bubble, combo, and radar.
Use chart blocks when the workbook includes a chart whose series, placement, and type should survive round-tripping.

title: Chart series lines
content: Series syntax: '- series <type> "<label>" cat=<range> val=<range> [size=<range>] [axis=primary|secondary] [color=#RRGGBB] [labels]'.
Use 'size=' only for bubble series, 'axis=secondary' for combo or dual-axis layouts, and 'labels' when data labels should be shown.
Example: '- series line "Margin %" cat=A4:A6 val=C4:C6 axis=secondary color=#C00000 labels'.

title: Chart type: column
content: Type: 'column'.
Meaning: vertical bars comparing values across categories.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. Example: '- series column "Revenue" cat=A4:A6 val=B4:B6'.

title: Chart type: bar
content: Type: 'bar'.
Meaning: horizontal bars comparing values across categories.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. It uses the same data model as column, just rotated.

title: Chart type: line
content: Type: 'line'.
Meaning: connected points showing change across an ordered sequence.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. It is best when category order matters.

title: Chart type: area
content: Type: 'area'.
Meaning: a filled line chart emphasizing magnitude over a sequence.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. It uses the same data model as line.

title: Chart type: pie
content: Type: 'pie'.
Meaning: part-to-whole slices from one series.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. The portable built-in case is one series.

title: Chart type: doughnut
content: Type: 'doughnut'.
Meaning: ring-style part-to-whole chart.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. The portable built-in case is one series.

title: Chart type: scatter
content: Type: 'scatter'.
Meaning: a numeric x/y point chart.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. In scatter charts, 'cat' is treated as the x-values.

title: Chart type: bubble
content: Type: 'bubble'.
Meaning: a scatter chart with a third size dimension.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range> size=<range>'. Example: '- series bubble "Risks" cat=B6:B20 val=C6:C20 size=D6:D20 color=#4472C4'.

title: Chart type: combo
content: Type: 'combo'.
Meaning: a mixed chart made from per-series types.
Per-series type decides the shape, and combo charts commonly mix 'column' and 'line', often with 'axis=secondary' on the line series.

title: Chart type: radar
content: Type: 'radar'.
Meaning: categories placed around a radial axis.
Series shape: 'cat=<range> val=<range>'. It is useful for profile or score comparisons.

title: Sparkline directive
content: Syntax: '[spark <cell> source=<range> type=<type> [color=#RRGGBB] [negative=#RRGGBB]]'.
Built-in sparkline types are 'line', 'column', and 'winloss'.
Use sparklines for tiny in-cell trend graphics driven by a source range.

title: Sparkline type: line
content: Type: 'line'.
Meaning: a tiny line chart showing movement across the source values.
Example: '[spark D4 source=B4:C4 type=line color=#4472C4]'.

title: Sparkline type: column
content: Type: 'column'.
Meaning: a tiny bar chart with one vertical bar per source value.
Use it for compact magnitude comparison when a line sparkline would be less clear.

title: Sparkline type: winloss
content: Type: 'winloss'.
Meaning: a tiny positive/negative outcome chart.
Positive values are wins, negative values are losses, and zero is neutral.

title: Length and size units
content: Built-in length units are 'px', 'pt', 'in', 'cm', and 'mm'.
Use these units in places such as chart sizes like 'size=720x360px'.
Keep sizes compact and only specify them when the workbook feature actually needs an explicit footprint.

title: Canonical serialization rules
content: Canonical order is: workbook attributes, shared directives (style, name), sheets in workbook order, sheet directives (state, view, used), shared range directives (type, fmt), anchored row lines, explicit '[cell ...]' overrides, then standalone non-cell directives (merge, validate, cf, table, pivot, chart, spark).

Compact export rules are: trim outer blank framing when safe, keep internal blanks with 'blank', omit formatting that only restates defaults, omit default alignment such as 'align=left/middle', hoist repeated non-default formatting into row attrlists or '[fmt ...]', hoist repeated adjacent temporal kinds into '[type ...]', and prefer row lines over '[cell ...]'.
Keep style metadata only when it is useful to downstream agents or required for fidelity.
